name: Ahuizotl
description: The Ahuizotl is a mythical creature from Aztec mythology, described as a dog-like creature with smooth, wet fur, small ears, and a long, prehensile hand at the end of its tail. This hand is used to snatch victims from the water's edge. The Ahuizotl is said to inhabit bodies of water, particularly those near settlements, and is believed to be a servant of the rain god Tlaloc. It lures victims to the water by mimicking the sound of a crying human child. Those who approach are dragged underwater and drowned. It is believed that Tlaloc then takes the souls of the drowned to his paradise, Tlalocan. The Ahuizotl is considered a dangerous omen, and its appearance is seen as a sign of impending death. While its physical description varies slightly in different accounts, the prehensile hand and its association with drowning are consistent features. The Ahuizotl is a significant figure in Aztec folklore, representing the dangers lurking in the natural world and the power of the gods.
subtitle: Water dog with a grasping hand
appearance: Dog-like creature with smooth, wet fur, small ears, and a long, prehensile hand at the end of its tail. Sometimes described as having spiky fur and human-like hands and feet. Often depicted with dark fur.
habitat: Lakes, rivers, and other bodies of water near human settlements.
behavior: Lures victims to the water's edge by mimicking the sound of a crying human child. Once close enough, the Ahuizotl uses its prehensile tail to drag the victim underwater and drown them. It is said to then present the bodies to Tlaloc.

keyMyths.title: Luring Victims
keyMyths.description: The Ahuizotl lures people to their deaths by imitating the cries of a baby, a tactic used to attract unsuspecting individuals to the water's edge. Once close enough, it uses its tail to grab and drown them. The drowned are supposedly sent to Tlalocan.
keyMyths.source: Various Aztec codices and oral traditions

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain (Florentine Codex)
sources.description: Provides a detailed description of the Ahuizotl and its habits.
